claim_text: SSIM is a combined measure of the luminance, contrast, and structure of the image given by^45 SSIM(x, x̂) = l(x, x̂) · c(x, x̂) · s(x, x̂),

sub_claims.text: SSIM is defined in zhouwang2004 as a combined measure of luminance, contrast, and structure comparisons, with the simplified product form SSIM(x, y) = l(x, y) · c(x, y) · s(x, y) (equivalent to the general weighted form when alpha = beta = gamma = 1).

evidence.snippet: Under the assumption that human visual perception is highly adapted for extracting structural information from a scene, we introduce an alternative framework for quality assessment based on the degradation of structural information. As a specific example of this concept, we develop a Structural Similarity Index and demonstrate its promise through a set of intuitive examples, as well as comparison to both subjective ratings and state-of-the-art objective methods on a database of images compressed with JPEG and JPEG2000.

In Section III, we describe a new paradigm for quality assessment, based on the hypothesis that the HVS is highly adapted for extracting structural information. As a specific example, we develop a measure of structural similarity that compares local patterns of pixel intensities that have been normalized for luminance and contrast.

The system separates the task of similarity measurement into three comparisons: luminance, contrast and structure. First, the luminance of each signal is compared. Assuming discrete signals, this is estimated as the mean intensity...

Finally, we combine the three comparisons of Eqs. (6), (9) and (10) and name the resulting similarity measure the Structural SIMilarity (SSIM) index between signals x and y: ... where alpha > 0, beta > 0 and gamma > 0 are parameters used to adjust the relative importance of the three components.

It is easy to verify that this definition satisfies the three conditions given above. In order to simplify the expression, we set alpha = beta = gamma = 1 and C3 = C2/2 in this paper. This results in a specific form of the SSIM index...

sub_claims.nuance: Section III.B explicitly defines SSIM as the combination of luminance, contrast, and structure comparisons; the manuscript's product form l · c · s matches the source's simplified case (alpha = beta = gamma = 1) given immediately after the general weighted definition in zhouwang2004. The paper credits earlier conference work (UQI, Wang & Bovik refs [6]-[8]) as a precursor that this paper generalizes, but the SSIM name and canonical journal-form definition originate in this 2004 TIP paper, so attribution is correct.

phrasings_tried: we develop a Structural Similarity Index
SSIM = l · c · s product form (alpha = beta = gamma = 1)

attestation.indirect_attribution_check: Section III.B and III.A note an earlier instantiation of the structural-similarity approach in Wang & Bovik refs [6]-[8] (the 'universal quality index' UQI; e.g., Wang & Bovik 2002 SPL), which the present paper 'generalizes.' However, the formal SSIM index, including the name 'Structural SIMilarity (SSIM) index' and the canonical luminance/contrast/structure formulation (and its simplified product form l · c · s used by the manuscript), is explicitly defined in this 2004 TIP paper. Citing zhouwang2004 is the standard primary attribution; not INDIRECT_SOURCE.
attestation.out_of_context_check: No context mismatch. The paper defines SSIM as the product of luminance, contrast, and structure comparisons under the alpha = beta = gamma = 1 simplification used throughout the paper; the manuscript cites the same product form for the same purpose (a perceptual image-quality metric).
